concepts.title: Critical Points
concepts.description: Critical points are the points where the derivative of the function is zero or undefined. They are usually the points where the function changes its behavior.

concepts.title: Increasing/Decreasing Intervals
concepts.description: To determine the increasing/decreasing intervals, find the critical points and test the sign of the derivative in each interval. If the derivative is positive, the function is increasing, and if it's negative, the function is decreasing.

concepts.title: Maxima/Minima
concepts.description: To find maxima/minima, find the critical points and use the second derivative test. If the second derivative is positive, it's a minima, and if it's negative, it's a maxima.

concepts.title: Second Derivative Test Mnemonic
concepts.description: To remember the second derivative test, use the mnemonic 'Positive Second Derivative Test: Positive means Minus' (PSDPM). This means if the second derivative is positive, it's a minima.

concepts.title: Critical Points vs Turning Points
concepts.description: Students often confuse critical points with turning points. Critical points are where the derivative is zero or undefined, while turning points are where the function changes its direction.

concepts.title: Rolle's Theorem
concepts.description: Rolle's theorem states that if a function f(x) is continuous on the closed interval [a, b] and differentiable on the open interval (a, b), and if f(a) = f(b), then there exists a point c in (a, b) where f'(c) = 0.

concepts.title: Lagrange's Mean Value Theorem
concepts.description: Lagrange's mean value theorem states that if a function f(x) is continuous on the closed interval [a, b] and differentiable on the open interval (a, b), then there exists a point c in (a, b) where f'(c) = (f(b) - f(a)) / (b - a).
